detailed_analysis: mcp_secret_policy.py implements a key-name-driven heuristic for detecting literal secrets inside per-agent MCP server configs, plus three policy modes (refuse | redact | permissive) and env-reference detection. The structure is small, modular, and well-typed; correctness/structure are largely sound. However, as a security-sensitive heuristic it has several gaps that an attacker — or a routine user mistake — can leverage: (a) the 'safe env-reference' regex is anchored, so any concatenated or prefixed env reference (e.g. 'prefix-${env:TOKEN}') becomes a 'literal' and either trips refuse or gets silently rewritten in redact mode, destroying user data; (b) the 'safe' regex requires UPPER_SNAKE env var names and silently mis-treats lowercase references; (c) two large allowlist branches — keys ending in 'env_var'/'env_vars' and ANY path containing 'env_http_headers' — trust key names without validating values, so a literal placed under those keys passes through unredacted under all three policies; (d) the SECRET_FIELD_RE regex over key names lacks Unicode normalization (NFKC), so homoglyph keys bypass detection; (e) the regex misses common secret-bearing field names ('passphrase', 'credential', 'credentials', 'dsn', 'connection_string', 'cookie', 'jwt', 'pat', 'private_key') and inspects only key names, never values, so no entropy/length sanity check exists; (f) module-level mutable cache for permissive warnings couples tests to a reset hook; (g) the special-case branch for ('auth','client_secret') is dead — already caught by SECRET_FIELD_RE on 'secret'. None of these are critical injection or hardcoded-secret defects in the module itself, but the over-permissive defaults make 'refuse' weaker than its name implies. The redact placeholders are per-call indexed ('AGENTS_SYNC_REDACTED_1') with no original_env_var preserved, which is destructive and gives the operator no way to map a placeholder back to the intended secret. test_mcp_real_adapters.py claims 'end-to-end coverage for real Claude/Codex/OpenCode MCP adapters' but only one of its six tests actually exercises the secret policy, and that test only confirms the placeholder appears in the rendered output — it never asserts the original literal ('literal-token') is ABSENT from any output file. The five remaining tests use env-reference inputs that the heuristic is explicitly designed to allow; they cannot detect any regression in literal-secret detection.

findings.problem: The env-name character class is hardcoded to UPPER_SNAKE. POSIX env var names allow mixed case. A user value of '${env:my_token}' fails the 'safe reference' check and falls into _is_secret_literal as a 'literal'. Under refuse: triggers McpSecretLeakError. Under redact: silently rewritten to a placeholder, losing intent.

Both safe-reference regexes are fully anchored: the entire value must be a single env reference. Values that legitimately combine an env reference with surrounding text ('prefix-${env:TOKEN}', '${env:USER}:${env:PASSWORD}', 'Basic ${env:BASIC_AUTH}') are treated as literal secrets. Under redact the entire string is replaced, destroying the prefix/suffix the user intended to keep.

Two allowlist branches trust key names without inspecting values: (a) any key ending in 'env_var' or 'env_vars' is declared safe; (b) any path that has 'env_http_headers' as any component is declared safe. A literal secret accidentally (or maliciously) placed under bearer_token_env_var='ghp_xxxx' or under env_http_headers.X-Auth='literal-token' bypasses detection under ALL three policies — including 'refuse'.

findings.variant: Incomplete secret-field name dictionary; no Unicode normalization
findings.problem: Only four token roots are matched: api[_-]?key, token, secret, password. Common secret-bearing field names that are missed: passphrase, credential, credentials, dsn, connection_string, cookie, session, jwt, pat, private_key. key.lower() does NOT apply unicode.normalize('NFKC'); homoglyphs bypass. No value-side entropy / known-prefix detection (ghp_, sk-, AKIA, xoxb-, eyJ).
The special-case check for path == ('auth','client_secret') is unreachable: when key == 'client_secret', SECRET_FIELD_RE matches the substring 'secret' on the next line.

This branch fires only when 'headers' is at the TOP level (depth 2). If a future caller passes the full Claude file ({'mcpServers': {<name>: {'headers': {...}}}}) into apply_mcp_secret_policy, the Authorization header path becomes length 4 and this branch silently stops matching.
Module-level mutable set, mutated from apply_mcp_secret_policy and only cleared via an explicit reset function. Hidden state shared across all callers. Cache keys are unbounded.

findings.variant: Destructive placeholder with no recoverable mapping
findings.problem: Placeholders are per-call positional ('_1', '_2', …) and the redaction record sets original_env_var=None unconditionally. The redact mode therefore destroys information without offering any forensic mapping back to which secret was at which slot.
The function returns the input dict by reference for 'no findings' and 'permissive', but returns a deep-copied dict for 'redact'. Callers cannot rely on aliasing or non-aliasing semantics.

findings.variant: Unvalidated name passthrough into format string
findings.problem: name is interpolated directly into the rendered env-reference string without validating against _ENV_NAME.

findings.variant: List-index path component encoded as bare str
findings.problem: List indices are stringified into the path tuple, indistinguishable from dict keys.

findings.variant: Test pins presence of placeholder but not absence of secret
findings.problem: This is the only test in the file that exercises the secret policy, and it only asserts that the placeholder appears at one known field. A regression where the secret 'literal-token' leaks elsewhere — into a comment, sidecar, opencode output (not even read), canonical state, or log output — would pass this test.

findings.variant: Tests that cannot detect a secret-policy regression
findings.problem: Five of six tests use env-reference inputs under default 'refuse'. If _is_secret_literal were changed to 'return False' for all keys, every one of these tests would still pass.

findings.variant: Refuse policy never exercised end-to-end with a real literal
findings.problem: No test feeds a literal-secret input through Syncer().sync_once() under 'refuse' and asserts the documented behavior.

findings.variant: Missing assertion on canonical state round-trip
findings.problem: The test reads only the rendered Claude and Codex files. Never opens state.json, never verifies secret_redactions metadata, opencode also unverified.

findings.variant: Test relies on undocumented json-then-tomllib parser fallback
findings.problem: json.loads does not accept // comments or trailing commas. If this test passes today it does so via _loads_slot's silent json-then-tomllib fallback.
Two-letter directory codes are magic strings whose meaning lives only in the call-site mapping.

verdict_summary: No critical hardcoded-secrets or injection defects, but the secret heuristic has structural gaps (over-permissive key-suffix allowlists, lowercase/concatenated env-ref blind spots, no Unicode normalization, missing common secret field names) and the 'real adapters' test file only exercises the secret policy once and never asserts the literal is actually absent — together making 'refuse' weaker than its name and the tests unable to catch a real leak regression.
